audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 15, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Maximum audit for Amalek memory, herem vocabulary, command to kill infants and animals, Kenite separation, Saul's selective destruction and spoil, Divine regret, sacrifice/obedience rhetoric, fear of people, robe sign, theological change-of-mind tension, Agag speech, Samuel's dismemberment, and genocide-misuse safeguards.

verses.restored: Samuel said to Saul, YHWH sent me to anoint you as king over his people Israel. Now listen to voice of words of YHWH.
Thus says YHWH of armies: I have attended to what Amalek did to Israel, how he set himself against him on way when he came up from Egypt.
Now go and strike Amalek, and devote to destruction all that belongs to him. Do not spare him, but put to death man and woman, child and nursing infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey.
Saul summoned people and mustered them at Telaim: two hundred thousand foot soldiers and ten thousand men of Judah.
Saul came to city of Amalek and lay in wait in wadi.

Saul said to Kenites, Go, turn aside, come down from midst of Amalekites, lest I destroy you with them; for you showed covenant-kindness to all children of Israel when they came up from Egypt. Kenites turned aside from midst of Amalek.
Saul struck Amalek from Havilah as far as Shur, which is before Egypt.
He seized Agag king of Amalek alive, and all people he devoted to destruction by edge of sword.

But Saul and people spared Agag and best of sheep, cattle, second-born/fatlings, lambs, and all that was good, and they were unwilling to devote them to destruction; all despised and weak property they devoted.
Word of YHWH came to Samuel, saying,
I regret that I made Saul king, for he has turned back from following me and has not established my words. Samuel was angry/distressed, and he cried to YHWH all night.
Samuel rose early to meet Saul in morning. It was told to Samuel, saying, Saul came to Carmel, and look, he set up for himself a monument; then he turned and crossed over and went down to Gilgal.
Samuel came to Saul, and Saul said to him, Blessed are you of YHWH; I have established word of YHWH.
Samuel said, Then what is this sound of sheep in my ears and sound of cattle that I hear?
Saul said, From Amalekites they brought them, for people spared best of sheep and cattle to sacrifice to YHWH your Divine, and rest we devoted to destruction.
Samuel said to Saul, Stop, and I will tell you what YHWH spoke to me this night. He said to him, Speak.
Samuel said, Though you were small in your own eyes, are you not head of tribes of Israel? YHWH anointed you as king over Israel.
YHWH sent you on a way and said, Go, devote sinners, Amalek, to destruction, and fight against them until they are consumed.
Why did you not listen to voice of YHWH? Why did you swoop upon spoil and do evil in eyes of YHWH?
Saul said to Samuel, I did listen to voice of YHWH, and I went on way YHWH sent me. I brought Agag king of Amalek, and Amalek I devoted to destruction.
People took from spoil sheep and cattle, best of devoted things, to sacrifice to YHWH your Divine at Gilgal.
Samuel said, Does YHWH delight in ascending offerings and sacrifices as in listening to voice of YHWH? Look, listening is better than sacrifice, and attending than fat of rams.
For rebellion is as sin of divination, and stubbornness as iniquity and teraphim. Because you rejected word of YHWH, he has rejected you from being king.
Saul said to Samuel, I have sinned, for I crossed mouth of YHWH and your words, because I feared people and listened to their voice.
Now lift, please, my sin and return with me, that I may bow down to YHWH.
Samuel said to Saul, I will not return with you, for you rejected word of YHWH, and YHWH has rejected you from being king over Israel.
As Samuel turned to go, Saul seized wing of his robe, and it tore.
Samuel said to him, YHWH has torn kingdom of Israel from you this day and given it to your companion who is better than you.
Also Glory/Endurance of Israel does not lie and does not change mind like a human, for he is not a human that he should change mind.
Saul said, I have sinned; now honor me, please, before elders of my people and before Israel, and return with me, that I may bow down to YHWH your Divine.
Samuel returned after Saul, and Saul bowed down to YHWH.
Samuel said, Bring near to me Agag king of Amalek. Agag came to him delicately/cheerfully, and Agag said, Surely bitterness of death has turned aside.
Samuel said, As your sword has bereaved women, so your mother shall be bereaved among women. Samuel cut Agag in pieces before YHWH at Gilgal.
Samuel went to Ramah, and Saul went up to his house at Gibeah of Saul.
Samuel did not see Saul again until day of his death, but Samuel grieved over Saul. And YHWH regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel.

translation_notes: This chapter contains an explicit command to kill an entire population, including infants and animals. It is a genocide text and requires maximum ethical and textual scrutiny.
'Amalek' may not be mapped onto any modern ethnicity, religion, nation, political opponent, or inherited enemy. No living group may be targeted through this narrative.
The Kenites' removal shows differentiation within the story but does not make collective extermination ethical.
Saul's offense in the narrative is selective noncompliance and spoil-taking, not excessive mercy toward civilians; interpreters must not conclude that compassion is sinful.
Herem/devotion-to-destruction is ancient war theology, not a lawful or spiritual practice for modern people.
Samuel's 'listening is better than sacrifice' critiques ritual used to cover disobedience and exploitation; it must not become a tool for authoritarian obedience to human leaders.
Saul's fear of the people and concern for public honor expose unstable leadership, but confession under political pressure should still be handled without humiliation or violence.
Robe-tearing becomes a dynastic sign; physical grabbing is reported, not normalized.
Verses 11 and 35 say YHWH regrets, while verse 29 says the Glory of Israel does not change mind. The theological tension must be preserved rather than flattened.
Samuel's execution and dismemberment of Agag are brutal retaliatory killing. 'Before YHWH' does not make torture or execution ethically clean.
Agag's prior violence is named through bereaved women; accountability for a ruler's violence does not authorize cruelty, collective punishment, or desecration of a body.
Samuel grieves Saul even after rupture. Moral judgment does not erase relationship, sorrow, or Saul's humanity.
safety_note: Maximum safeguard: This genocide text must never authorize killing, expulsion, collective punishment, ethnic hatred, war crimes, harm to children, or violence against animals. Never identify a modern people as Amalek. Do not teach that mercy is disobedience. Samuel's killing of Agag is reported brutality, not a sacred execution model.
